name: Wepwawet
description: Wepwawet, whose name translates to 'Opener of the Ways,' was an ancient Egyptian wolf-headed deity. He was primarily associated with warfare, hunting, and funeral processions, acting as a guide for the deceased in the afterlife. Often depicted as a wolf or jackal, or as a man with the head of a wolf or jackal, Wepwawet played a crucial role in ensuring safe passage and triumph. He originated in Upper Egypt, specifically in the city of Asyut (Lycopolis), and was venerated as a protector and scout for the army. Over time, Wepwawet became linked to the royal ideology, representing the pharaoh's power and ability to conquer enemies. His association with Anubis, the jackal-headed god of embalming and the dead, led to a syncretism of their roles, with Wepwawet sometimes depicted leading the way during funeral processions and guiding the deceased through the underworld. He possessed the power to overcome obstacles, clear paths, and provide protection in both the physical and spiritual realms. His military association also gave him the power to ensure victory in battle. Wepwawet's importance lies in his role as a facilitator, ensuring the smooth transition between worlds and guaranteeing success in endeavors.
subtitle: Opener of the Ways, Divine Scout

keyMyths.title: Wepwawet Leads the Army
keyMyths.description: Wepwawet is said to have appeared before the Egyptian army in the form of a wolf, leading them to victory against their enemies. His presence instilled courage and ensured their triumph. This solidified his role as a protector of the military and a symbol of royal power.

keyMyths.title: Guidance in the Afterlife
keyMyths.description: As 'Opener of the Ways,' Wepwawet guided the souls of the deceased through the perilous journey of the underworld. He cleared obstacles and protected them from harm, ensuring their safe arrival in the afterlife. This role linked him closely with funerary rituals and beliefs.

keyMyths.title: Connection to Anubis
keyMyths.description: While distinct, Wepwawet's role as a guide in funerary processions often saw him associated with Anubis. Sometimes, Wepwawet was depicted leading the way for Anubis, highlighting their complementary roles in the realm of death and the afterlife. This connection strengthened their shared association with the deceased.

sources.author: Richard H. Wilkinson
sources.work: Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt

sources.author: Emily Teeter
sources.work: Religion and Ritual in Ancient Egypt
